items.text: The CPUC conducts a GRC for the Utility every four years.
items.rationale: States a routine regulatory process fact (GRC every four years) with no new risk implication.

items.text: Diablo Canyon Extended Operations In lieu of the traditional rate-based return on investment, the Utility receives a fixed payment of $100 million plus a volumetric payment of $13 per MWh generated by DCPP.
items.rationale: Describes a specific new compensation mechanism for DCPP replacing traditional rate-based return, a material financial structure change.

items.text: The fixed payment may be adjusted downward in the event of extended unplanned outages.
items.rationale: Adds detail that the fixed payment can be reduced for outages, extending the existing DCPP financial risk.

items.text: If the Utility’s costs average less than 99% of a market-based benchmark, then the Utility returns 80% of such savings to customers, subject to a cap; if the Utility’s costs average more than 102% of the benchmark, the Utility recovers 50% of such excess costs.
items.rationale: Provides specific mechanics of cost sharing risk tied to benchmark performance, expanding on cost recovery risk.

items.text: The automatic extension does not cover general wage increases, which must be separately bargained and agreed to for 2026 and beyond.
items.rationale: Introduces specific uncertainty that wage increases for 2026+ require separate bargaining, a new labor negotiation risk.

items.text: The Employee Resource Groups are open to all employees.
items.rationale: General statement about employee resource group openness, not risk-related.

items.text: In 2025, the Utility estimated total net deliveries of electricity to retail customers were 24,052 GWh.
items.rationale: Statistical disclosure of delivery volume, factual data not a risk statement.

items.text: This amount represents the total amount of electricity generated and procured, net of electricity sold into the CAISO open market or to third parties.
items.rationale: Clarifies definition of delivery metric, purely descriptive.

items.text: Utility-owned resources generated approximately 60% of its net delivered electricity.
items.rationale: Statistical fact about generation mix, no risk framing.

items.text: Of the 2025 estimated total net deliveries of electricity to retail customers from generated and procured resources, approximately 71% was generated from GHG-free resources (34% qualifying renewable energy resources, 32% nuclear, and 5% large hydroelectric), and 29% was generated from natural gas generation resources.
items.rationale: Detailed statistical breakdown of energy sources, factual disclosure.

items.text: The Utility’s percentage of GHG-free generation decreased in 2025, compared to 2024, because DCPP’s generation became attributable to all customers statewide (rather than only the Utility’s customers).
items.rationale: Explains a change in GHG-free percentage due to regulatory attribution shift, expanding on existing generation mix risk context.

items.text: This change does not represent a decrease in the Utility’s ownership of the DCPP resource; rather, the generation associated with this resource became attributed among other LSEs’ portfolios.
items.rationale: Clarifies that the change is not a decrease in ownership, restating prior point without new risk.

items.text: 23 The Utility calculates net deliveries of electricity according to the Power Content Label methodology based on CEC guidelines.
items.rationale: Describes calculation methodology, procedural detail.

items.text: In September 2025 the CPUC also conditionally authorized the Utility to recover the costs, up to a cap, associated with increasing the nameplate generating capacity of its Helms Pumped Storage Facility.
items.rationale: Adds new regulatory authorization detail regarding cost recovery for Helms facility, extending capital cost risk.

items.text: The Utility’s ability to accurately predict the location and pace of electric load growth is limited, due to factors such as extent of customer demand, the policy environment, and macroeconomics.
items.rationale: Introduces a specific new risk about the Utility's limited ability to predict load growth location and pace.

items.text: Load growth can reduce other customers' rates when the incremental revenue for the new load is greater than the incremental cost to serve that load.
items.rationale: Identifies a new specific risk/benefit dynamic where load growth can affect other customers' rates.

items.text: The degree to which load growth reduces other customers’ rates will depend on the pricing for the new load, which in turn depends on the unit cost of power for the new load, the costs to construct infrastructure to connect new load, the Utility’s cost to serve the new load, and the amount of power used.
items.rationale: Elaborates on factors influencing the rate impact risk introduced in the prior sentence.

items.text: The Utility is engaged with regulators and other stakeholders on policies, such as cost allocation and rate design frameworks, that support conditions for load growth to improve affordability for customers.
items.rationale: Describes ongoing stakeholder engagement, a mitigating/descriptive statement rather than a risk.

items.text: In 2025, the changes to state law authorized the program through 2045.
items.rationale: States a regulatory program extension date, factual update.

items.text: Complying entities may also satisfy a portion of their compliance obligation through the purchase of offset credits.
items.rationale: Describes an existing compliance option (offset credits), procedural detail.

items.text: The Utility is currently developing the next CAVA, which is expected to be more granular than the previous climate vulnerability assessment and will be submitted to the CPUC in 2027.
items.rationale: Describes future planning activity (CAVA update), procedural/forward-looking statement without new risk.

items.text: The Utility works to incorporate scientific information into its operations by reviewing relevant scientific literature.
items.rationale: General statement about incorporating scientific literature, not a risk.

items.text: Measuring emissions data involves complex estimates and assumptions, which may change as a result of methodology changes.
items.rationale: Highlights uncertainty in emissions measurement methodology, expanding on data accuracy risk.

items.text: 31 The Utility achieved a third-party verified CO 2 emissions rate of 16 pounds of CO 2 per MWh for electricity delivered to retail customers in 2024, using the CEC’s Power Source Disclosure program methodology.
items.rationale: Reports a verified emissions statistic, factual disclosure.

items.text: Furthermore, for the Continuation Account to be available for payment of eligible claims, the Wildfire Fund administrator must determine that the Continuation Account is necessary, the CPUC must authorize extending the non-bypassable charge, and there must be sufficient funds remaining in the Continuation Account.
items.rationale: Adds specific conditions required for Continuation Account availability, expanding existing wildfire fund risk.

items.text: PG&E Corporation and the Utility are also unable to predict whether the administrator will determine that additional contributions are needed, and if so, the timing of those contingent contributions.
items.rationale: Expands uncertainty about additional contingent contributions related to the wildfire fund.

items.text: See “Key Factors Affecting Financial Results” and “Critical Accounting Estimates” in Item 7.
items.rationale: Cross-reference to other sections, not a risk statement.

items.text: Similarly, PG&E Corporation’s and the Utility’s costs to resolve the Wildfire-Related Securities Claims could exceed their estimated liabilities.
items.rationale: Notes potential for costs to exceed estimated liabilities, expanding existing securities litigation risk.

items.text: A hearing on the status of restitution in the Butte County District Attorney’s Office’s investigation into the 2018 Camp fire has been continued several times, most recently to April 24, 2026.
items.rationale: Provides a scheduling update on a legal proceeding, factual detail.

items.text: An Enhanced Oversight and Enforcement Process proceeding could result in the Utility losing its license to operate as a utility.
items.rationale: Introduces a severe new risk that an oversight proceeding could result in loss of the utility license.

items.text: For example, the CCPA requires a business to implement reasonable security procedures to safeguard personal information against unauthorized access, use, or disclosure.
items.rationale: Describes a regulatory requirement (CCPA security procedures), procedural/legal description.

items.text: 35 The Utility has been and could in the future be subject to regulatory or governmental enforcement actions with respect to its compliance with such rules.
items.rationale: Adds specificity about potential regulatory enforcement actions tied to compliance, expanding existing regulatory risk.

items.text: These investigations or enforcement actions could result in a judgment against the Utility.
items.rationale: Extends prior enforcement risk by noting potential judgments against the Utility.

items.text: Failure to comply with laws and regulations could result in material fines, penalties, customer refunds, other payments, increased oversight, and changes in the Utility’s operations and business model, reputational harm, and other negative consequences.
items.rationale: Generic list of possible negative consequences from non-compliance, standard risk boilerplate.

items.text: The Utility also depends on information technology systems to help it monitor and operate the electric grid, detect ignitions and collect other wildfire-related information, process transactions, track and collect revenues, manage customer billing and energy usage data, maintain internal control over financial reporting, and produce accurate and timely financial statements and regulatory filings.
items.rationale: Details specific operational functions dependent on IT systems, expanding existing cybersecurity/operational risk.

items.text: These information technology systems allow the Utility to create, collect, use, disclose, store, and otherwise process sensitive information, including regarding customers, employees, and other individuals.
items.rationale: Expands on data processing scope, adding detail to existing data privacy/security risk.

items.text: These systems can be damaged or disrupted by malicious events such as cyber or physical attacks, or by technology failure.
items.rationale: Restates the known risk of system damage from cyber/physical attacks or tech failure without new specifics.

items.text: Accordingly, the Utility may not be able to prevent unauthorized access to its operational networks, information technology systems or data, or the disruption of its operations.
items.rationale: Reiterates inability to guarantee prevention of unauthorized access, consistent with prior risk language.

items.text: The systems and networks of PG&E Corporation and the Utility may also be damaged or disrupted by technology failures due to errors in software or platforms or the inability to appropriately support, update, expand, recover or integrate technology within PG&E Corporation and the Utility’s networks.
items.rationale: Adds detail about technology failure causes, expanding existing IT risk disclosure.

items.text: The Utility is engaged in complex projects regarding its billing and enterprise resource planning systems.
items.rationale: Introduces a new specific operational risk regarding complex billing and ERP system projects.
